screen.name: screen1
screen.instruct:
First we need to extract the DNA from the bloodstain samples.
Open both containers and take out the swabs, then use the scissors to cut both of them.

screen.name: screen2
screen.instruct:
Great. I’ve labeled two tubes filled with lysis buffer with F for “floor” and W for “wall”.
Use the tweezers to transfer the swabs to the corresponding tubes.

screen.name: screen3
screen.instruct:
The lysis buffer breaks the blood cells apart and sets the DNA free.
That takes about 30 minutes.
screen.caption: DNA
Blood cell
Cell particle

screen.name: screen4
screen.instruct:
Next, transfer the swabs over to the empty tubes.
We’ll keep them just in case something goes wrong with the analysis.

screen.name: screen5
screen.instruct: Now place both samples in the centrifuge to separate the DNA in the fluid from the broken cell material.

screen.name: screen6
screen.instruct: As the sample is centrifuged, the broken-up cell particles move to the bottom of the tube and form a pellet, while the DNA stays in the supernatant, the fluid above the pellet.
screen.caption: DNA
Cell particle
Pellet
Supernatant

screen.name: screen7
screen.instruct: That’ll do. Place the tubes back in the tray.

screen.name: screen8
screen.instruct:
To clean the DNA, we’ll use special filters.
Use the pipette to transfer the supernatant, which contains the DNA, from both of your original tubes to the new tubes on the left.

screen.name: screen9
screen.instruct: Next, place the samples in the centrifuge and start the machine.

screen.name: screen10
screen.instruct: The fluid containing the DNA is forced through the filter due to the centrifugal force, but only the DNA molecules stick to the filter material.
screen.caption: DNA
Filter

screen.name: screen11
screen.instruct: Take out the samples and place them in the tray.

screen.name: screen12
screen.instruct: Next, take the filters from both of the tubes you’ve just centrifuged and place them in the corresponding empty tubes on the right.

screen.name: screen13
screen.instruct:
The last step of the extraction process is to remove the cleaned DNA from the filters with fresh eluting buffer.
Use the pipette to transfer a specific amount of eluting buffer to both samples.

screen.name: screen14
screen.instruct: The eluting buffer removes the DNA from the filter.
screen.caption: DNA
Filter

screen.name: screen15
screen.instruct: Great. Put the empty filters in the tubes on the left. We’ll store them just in case.
screen.label: DNA filter F
DNA filter W

screen.name: screen16
screen.instruct:
Now that the DNA is extracted from the blood cells, you’re ready to amplify the DNA. We’re going to do a PCR, Polymerase Chain Reaction.
First, transfer a specific amount of the replicating solution to both samples.
screen.label: Replicating solution

screen.name: screen17
screen.instruct:
The polymerase enzyme in the replicating solution will make thousands of copies of the DNA in our sample.
To start this 2-hour process, transfer the samples to the PCR machine and press start.

screen.name: screen18
screen.instruct:
Since we don’t want to copy all of the DNA, the PCR is set up so that it only copies 13 specific DNA markers.
DNA markers, also called Short Tandem Repeats (STR), are regions on the DNA that are unique in every person.
screen.caption: DNA
Marker

screen.name: screen19
screen.instruct: The amplification is done. Place the samples back in the tray.

screen.name: screen20
screen.instruct:
Next, we need to separate and sort the DNA markers. The genetic analyzer is going to take care of that.
Place our samples in the genetic analyzer.

screen.name: screen21
screen.instruct: Good. Now start the machine.

screen.name: screen22
screen.instruct:
In the genetic analyzer, the DNA markers are pushed through a small gel-filled tube. Small markers move through the gel faster than large markers.
A sensor then records the time the markers exit the tube.
screen.caption: Gel-filled tube
DNA marker

screen.name: screen23
screen.instruct: The results are in. Click on the computer to access them.

screen.name: screen24
screen.instruct:
Here is your first DNA profile, also called DNA fingerprint, from the blood you collected from the wall.
Click on the first marker to zoom in.

screen.name: screen25
screen.instruct: This marker is represented by two different peaks, because this person has inherited two different copies of this marker – one from the mother, one from the father.

screen.name: screen26
screen.instruct:
The number below each peak indicates how often this particular copy is present in the person’s DNA.
Click the marker to zoom out.

screen.name: screen27
screen.instruct: Click on another marker to zoom in.

screen.name: screen28
screen.instruct:
This marker has only one peak, because the two copies the person inherited from mother and father are the same.
Click on the marker again to zoom out.

screen.name: screen29
screen.instruct: Since each person has a different set AND different numbers of DNA markers, no two people have the same DNA profile.

screen.name: comparison
screen.instruct: Drag the DNA profile from the wall bloodstains to the left viewing area.
Drag the DNA profile from the floor bloodstains to the right viewing area.
Do these DNA profiles match?
screen.codisText: The Combined DNA Index Systems (CODIS) is a database of over 4 million DNA profiles. The profiles come from convicted felons, evidence, and missing persons.
